name: cheesy white bean tomato bake
instructions:
From Alison Roman (NYT Cooking). A skillet-to-oven bake built on fried tomato paste, white beans, and melted mozzarella. Simple, fast, deep-flavored — the whole thing is done in ~15 minutes.

INGREDIENTS (4 servings):
- 1/4 cup extra-virgin olive oil
- 3 fat garlic cloves, thinly sliced
- 3 tablespoons tomato paste
- 2 (15-oz) cans white beans (cannellini or great northern) or chickpeas, drained and rinsed
- 1/2 cup boiling water
- salt and black pepper
- 1/3 lb mozzarella, coarsely grated (~1 1/3 cups)

PROCEDURE:
1. Heat oven to 475°F.
2. In a 10-inch ovenproof skillet, heat the olive oil over medium-high. Fry the sliced garlic until lightly golden (~1 min).
3. FRY THE TOMATO PASTE: stir in the tomato paste (watch for splatter) and fry ~30 seconds, reducing heat as needed to prevent the garlic from burning. This is the load-bearing flavor step — see notes.
4. Add the drained beans, boiling water, and generous pinches of salt and pepper. Stir to combine.
5. Sprinkle the mozzarella evenly over the top.
6. Bake 5-10 min until the cheese has melted and browned in spots.
7. OPTIONAL FINISHING BROIL: if the top isn't as toasted as you'd like, run the skillet under the broiler for 1-2 minutes.
8. Serve at once, per Roman's headnote — with bread and a bitter-green salad.
notes:
=== ALISON ROMAN'S HEADNOTE (WHY THIS RECIPE EXISTS) ===

Roman's framing: this dish is 'for those of you who love lasagna's edges, where sticky tomato meets crisp cheese — even the middle.' The whole recipe is engineered to maximize the sticky-tomato-meets-crisp-cheese texture across every bite, not just the corners. The tube of tomato paste substitutes for the deep flavor of sun-dried tomato; frying it caramelizes the sugars and saturates the olive oil, giving you a mixture that glues onto everything you stir through it.

=== THE LOAD-BEARING TECHNIQUE — FRYING THE TOMATO PASTE ===

FRY THE TOMATO PASTE IN THE HOT GARLICKY OIL FOR 30 SECONDS: this is the whole trick. Raw tomato paste stirred into a dish stays flat and one-note. Fried tomato paste (30-60 sec in hot oil) caramelizes the natural sugars, drives off residual water, and dissolves into the oil as a concentrated red-orange coating — that oil now carries deep, almost sun-dried-tomato-adjacent flavor into anything you add next.

Don't skip and don't shortcut. Two failure modes:
- STIRRING TOMATO PASTE IN WITH THE BEANS/WATER: gives you a thin, unfried tomato-water base. The dish reads flat.
- FRYING TOO LONG / BURNING: tomato paste burns fast once the sugars caramelize past the sweet spot. Watch for the color to deepen from bright red to a rusty brick red; that's the finish line. Beyond that it goes bitter fast. Reduce heat as needed to keep the garlic from burning while the paste fries.

=== BOILING WATER, NOT COLD WATER ===

ADD BOILING WATER, NOT ROOM-TEMP OR COLD: the recipe calls specifically for boiling water. Cold water hitting the hot skillet drops the pan's temperature significantly, which risks the caramelized tomato paste re-hydrating in a cooled pan (losing some of its concentrated character) and pushes the whole dish out of the fast-oven-bake timeline. Boiling water keeps the pan hot and lets the tomato-paste + oil + water blend into a loose sauce immediately. Have the kettle already boiling before you start frying the garlic.

=== FORMAT — SKILLET TO OVEN, ~15 MIN TOTAL ===

This is not a slow-simmered bean dish. Every step is fast:
- Garlic in oil: 1 min
- Fry tomato paste: 30 sec
- Add beans + water + season: 30 sec of stirring
- Sprinkle cheese, bake at 475°F: 5-10 min
- Optional broil: 1-2 min

Total: ~10-15 minutes active. The high oven temp (475°F) is doing the sticky-tomato-meets-crisp-cheese work in the last stretch. Don't lower it 'to be safe' — you'll get soft cheese instead of the melted-and-browned-in-spots finish that the recipe is engineered around. The optional broiler pass is where you push into deliberately singed cheese if that's what you want; Roman leaves this to preference ('as molten or singed as you like').

=== WHITE BEANS OR CHICKPEAS — BOTH WORK ===

Roman explicitly allows white beans (cannellini, great northern) OR chickpeas. Cannellini gives the softest creamiest bite; great northern is slightly firmer; chickpeas are the firmest and add a nuttier register. All three work with the tomato-oil + mozzarella base. Cannellini is the default italian-white-bean choice; chickpeas push the dish toward a middle-eastern-adjacent read. Two cans total is what the recipe scales to — drain and rinse both.

=== VARIATIONS (PER ROMAN'S HEADNOTE) ===

Roman explicitly says: 'you could add in kale, noodles, even roasted vegetables.' The tomato-paste + oil base is designed to glom onto anything you stir through it, so the recipe is built to absorb additions. Some obvious ones:
- KALE: torn kale leaves added with the beans, wilted briefly before the cheese goes on. Adds a bitter-green counterweight to the rich tomato + cheese.
- COOKED NOODLES: short pasta shapes (rigatoni, penne, orecchiette) folded in with the beans, then baked. Turns this into a fast baked-pasta dish.
- ROASTED VEGETABLES: leftover roasted anything (cauliflower, squash, peppers, mushrooms) folded in. Uses the tomato-paste oil as a coating for what's already cooked.
All are secondary — the recipe as written is complete without them.

=== SERVING — BREAD AND A BITTER GREEN SALAD ===

Roman's pairing: bread (to sop the tomato-oil) and a bitter-green salad (arugula, radicchio, endives, chicory) to cut the richness. Follow both. The dish is one-note rich without bitter greens on the side; the bread is needed because the tomato-oil is too good to leave on the plate.

=== DELIBERATELY SPARE — CROSS-RECIPE NAVIGATION ===

The recipe is short on ingredients on purpose (7 items). If you want a dairy-forward version, creamy-italian-white-beans is the destination — cream + garlic breadcrumbs + parmesan + fresh herbs. Different dish, different flavor axis. This one is the tomato-and-cheese path.

=== BREADCRUMBS AS AN OPTIONAL FINISH ===

Sprinkle plain breadcrumbs alongside or over the mozzarella before baking for a crunchy gratin-style top. Works especially well if you spray/drizzle the crumbs with oil so they crisp under the high oven heat. Not in Roman's original but a natural addition — the recipe already has a sticky-cheese-crust top, and breadcrumbs add another textural layer without changing the flavor register.

=== SIBLING RECIPES ===

- creamy-italian-white-beans: the CREAM-and-breadcrumbs sibling. Also a fast white-bean side, but built on cream + garlic breadcrumbs + parmesan + fresh herbs instead of fried tomato paste + mozzarella. Reach for that when you want dairy-forward-white-beans; this one when you want tomato-and-cheese-forward.
- shakshuka: the eggs-in-tomato-sauce cousin. Tomato + skillet + baked topping. Different protein (eggs, not beans) and different tomato form (canned crushed, not paste), but the same 'skillet + oven finish + molten protein on top' skeleton.
- master-italian-tomato-sauce-and-pesto: the long-simmer italian tomato base recipes. If you want tomato as a slow-cooked sauce, that's the destination — cheesy-white-bean-tomato-bake is deliberately the fast one-pan version.
- lemony-shrimp-and-bean-stew: the SEAFOOD white-bean sibling (NYT Cooking). Same white-bean base, but built as a shrimp + leeks + lemon-butter-stock one-pot meal rather than a tomato-and-cheese bake. Reach for that when you want protein-forward; this one when you want tomato-and-cheese-forward.
